sections.Scene: The Roi de Deniers is the King of Coins in the Italian-suited Paris anonymous tarot. He appears enthroned with the large coin or denier as his suit emblem, the conventional seated king of the Italian pip-card tradition. The Paris court figures follow the standard Marseille-derived conventions for the coin suit's highest court rank.
sections.Symbol: Coin King enthroned — material authority and the governance of earthly wealth, the king who holds the suit's round emblem as his sceptre.

sections.Scene: The Reine de Deniers appears as the enthroned Queen of Coins. She holds or displays the coin emblem in the conventional seated-queen posture of the Italian-suited French tarot tradition. The Paris anonymous queen of coins follows the standard Marseille-derived iconography for the suit's feminine court rank.
sections.Symbol: Coin Queen enthroned — the fertile stewardship of material resources, wealth held in the feminine mode of reception and cultivation.

sections.Scene: The Cavalier de Deniers appears as a mounted knight with the coin emblem — a horseman displaying the suit's round disc in the conventional posture of the Italian-suited cavalier card. The mounted position distinguishes the cavalier from the standing valet in the Paris anonymous deck's Italian-tradition court hierarchy.
sections.Symbol: Coin Knight on horseback — material ambition in motion, wealth as an active pursuit carried forward on horseback.

sections.Scene: The Valet de Deniers is the page or jack of coins — a standing young figure holding or presenting the coin emblem. In the Italian-suited tradition this figure is the lowest of the four court ranks, a youth whose standing posture contrasts with the mounted cavalier and the enthroned senior courts above him.
sections.Symbol: Coin Valet standing — the apprentice of material affairs, youth in service to the suit's earthly values.

sections.Scene: The Dix de Deniers shows ten coin emblems distributed across the card in the conventional Italian pip arrangement. In the Paris anonymous tarot the coin pip cards follow the standard symmetrical distribution typical of 17th-century Italian-suited French playing cards, the circular coin shapes arranged in two-column or stacked formations.
sections.Symbol: Ten coins in full field — the suit at its most abundant, completed material wealth distributed across the card's full space.

sections.Scene: The Neuf de Deniers shows nine coins in their conventional pip arrangement. The nine-coin card approaches but does not reach the completeness of the ten, the nine circular emblems arranged in the standard Italian pip convention of the Paris anonymous tarot.
sections.Symbol: Nine coins approaching fullness — material near-completion, abundance one step from its final expression.

sections.Scene: The Huit de Deniers shows eight coins in a symmetrical pip arrangement. The eight-coin card has the geometric balance characteristic of even-number pip cards in the Italian-suited tradition, two columns of four or two rows of four evoking the stability of even distribution.
sections.Symbol: Eight coins in symmetric arrangement — the suit's measured and stable mid-range, material in its most organized form.

sections.Scene: The Sept de Deniers shows seven coins in the conventional pip arrangement. The odd number gives this card a slight visual asymmetry — one coin must sit off-center or alone — and in the Italian pip tradition the seven often places one coin above or below the central line to accommodate the odd count.
sections.Symbol: Seven coins in slight asymmetry — material abundance approaching its higher registers, the odd-number arrangement that marks an active rather than settled state.

sections.Scene: The Six de Deniers shows six coins in a balanced arrangement, typically two columns of three. The six-coin card is among the more open pip compositions of the suit, the even number allowing a harmonious distribution without the density of the higher pip counts.
sections.Symbol: Six coins in balanced columns — equitable distribution, material wealth at its point of harmonious measure and exchange.

sections.Scene: The Cinq de Deniers shows five coins in a cross or quincunx arrangement — four at the corners and one at center. The five-coin card introduces the odd-number instability characteristic of pip fives, the central coin establishing a focal point around which the other four cluster.
sections.Symbol: Five coins in cross arrangement — material asymmetry, the suit's tension concentrated in the odd central coin.

sections.Scene: The Quatre de Deniers shows four coins in a square formation. The four-coin pip card is among the most stable of the suit — the four-fold arrangement provides symmetry in both axes, evoking solidity and foundation in the Italian pip tradition.
sections.Symbol: Four coins in square — material stability and containment, the suit's four-fold structure as a foundation.

sections.Scene: The Trois de Deniers shows three coins in a triangular arrangement. The three-coin card introduces the minimal complexity of the Italian pip tradition — three is the first number that creates a spatial relationship beyond line or pair, the triangle of coins suggesting beginning organization.
sections.Symbol: Three coins in triangle — the beginning of organized material pattern, three points of wealth in active relation.

sections.Scene: The Deux de Deniers shows two coins placed vertically or diagonally in simple correspondence. In the Italian pip tradition the two-coin card is sometimes embellished with a ribbon or decorative flourish connecting the coins, the pairing of two circular emblems being the suit's most elemental statement of duality.
sections.Symbol: Two coins in correspondence — the primal material dyad, exchange and duality at its most elemental.

sections.Scene: The As de Deniers shows a single large coin as the suit's foundational emblem. In the Italian-suited tradition the aces often carry additional decorative treatment — the single coin given an elaborate surround or ornamental context that marks it as the seed of the suit. The Paris anonymous ace of coins is the concentrated essence of the denier symbol.
sections.Symbol: One coin as seed — the undivided material potential of the suit, wealth before it divides into distribution or exchange.

sections.What it is: The Deniers (Coins) suit — ten pips plus Valet, Cavalier, Reine and Roi.
sections.Scene: The suit of Deniers (Coins) in the Paris anonymous tarot uses the Italian-suited circular coin emblem with French court names: Roi, Reine, Cavalier, Valet. The pip cards follow standard Italian pip conventions with coin shapes in symmetrical distributions. This suit is part of the deck's Italian-suited structure within an otherwise distinctively French 17th-century production.
sections.Symbol: Coins as the suit of material wealth and earthly value — the circular emblem that measures and mediates human exchange, here within a deck that otherwise reimagines the Marseille tradition at every turn.

sections.Scene: The Roi de Coupes is the King of Cups in the Italian-suited Paris anonymous tarot. He appears enthroned with a chalice or cup as his suit emblem, the conventional seated king of the Italian pip-card tradition adapted to the cups suit. The cup king in the Paris anonymous deck follows the Marseille-derived iconography for the suit's highest court rank.
sections.Symbol: Cup King enthroned — emotional authority and the governance of inner life, the king who holds the chalice as his emblem of feeling and reception.

sections.Scene: The Reine de Coupes appears as the enthroned Queen of Cups, holding or displaying the chalice emblem in the conventional seated-queen posture. She is the feminine sovereign of the suit of feeling and inner life, the cups suit's highest feminine rank in the Paris anonymous tarot's Italian-suited court hierarchy.
sections.Symbol: Cup Queen enthroned — the feminine mastery of emotional intelligence, the queen who holds the chalice as both vessel and authority.

sections.Scene: The Cavalier de Coupes is the mounted Knight of Cups, a horseman holding or presenting the cup emblem. The cavalier's mounted position marks his active engagement with the suit's emotional sphere, the cup borne forward on horseback as an act of pursuit or offering.
sections.Symbol: Cup Knight on horseback — emotional quest in motion, feeling pursued and carried forward through the active mode of the mounted knight.

sections.Scene: The Valet de Coupes is the standing Page of Cups, a young figure presenting or holding the chalice. The valet is the lowest court rank in the Italian-suited hierarchy, his standing posture and youth marking him as servant rather than sovereign of the suit.
sections.Symbol: Cup Valet standing — the apprentice of emotional life, youth in service to the suit's depths of feeling and inner experience.

sections.Scene: The Dix de Coupes shows ten cups in the conventional Italian pip arrangement, the chalice or cup shapes distributed across the card in symmetrical formation. The ten-cup card marks the suit's fullness — all cups present, the emotional field complete.
sections.Symbol: Ten cups in full field — emotional completion, the suit at its most encompassing and abundant expression.

sections.Scene: The Neuf de Coupes shows nine cups in their conventional arrangement. The nine-cup card approaches the suit's fullness without completing it, a near-total field of chalices that suggests satisfaction held just short of its final form.
sections.Symbol: Nine cups approaching fullness — emotional near-fulfillment, the suit one step from its completed expression.

sections.Scene: The Huit de Coupes shows eight cups in a symmetrical pip arrangement. The even number allows a balanced two-column or two-row distribution of chalice emblems, the eight-cup card presenting the suit in its organized, stable mid-range.
sections.Symbol: Eight cups in balanced arrangement — emotional equilibrium, the suit's mid-point where feeling is held in stable and symmetric form.

sections.Scene: The Sept de Coupes shows seven cups distributed with the characteristic slight asymmetry of odd-number pip cards. Seven chalice shapes occupy the card's field, their odd count preventing perfect two-column symmetry and giving the card a quality of active emotional distribution.
sections.Symbol: Seven cups in slight asymmetry — emotional multiplicity, feeling distributed among seven distinct vessels that resist easy pairing.

sections.Scene: The Six de Coupes shows six cups in a balanced two-column arrangement. The open, even distribution of the six-cup card gives the suit a quality of harmonious exchange — six chalices sharing the field with generous spacing between them.
sections.Symbol: Six cups in balanced columns — emotional harmony and mutual exchange, the suit at its most open and receptive mid-point.

sections.Scene: The Cinq de Coupes shows five cups in a cross or quincunx arrangement. The odd-number asymmetry of the five cups introduces an element of instability or loss into the suit's emotional field — something spilled or absent, the quincunx suggesting both fullness and gap.
sections.Symbol: Five cups in cross arrangement — emotional asymmetry, the suit's tension around what is held and what is lost.

sections.Scene: The Quatre de Coupes shows four cups in a square formation. The four-cup card is the suit's most stable pip composition — the square arrangement of four chalices suggesting emotional containment and security, feeling held in a firm four-fold structure.
sections.Symbol: Four cups in square — emotional stability and containment, the suit's four-fold structure as a vessel of security.

sections.Scene: The Trois de Coupes shows three cups in a triangular arrangement. Three chalices in an implied triangle create the suit's first multi-point spatial relationship, the three cups suggesting a community or shared emotional ground rather than mere pairing.
sections.Symbol: Three cups in triangle — the beginning of emotional community, three vessels sharing a common field.

sections.Scene: The Deux de Coupes shows two cups placed in vertical or diagonal correspondence. The two-cup card is the suit's primal emotional dyad — two chalices facing or echoing each other, the minimal expression of emotional exchange and reciprocity.
sections.Symbol: Two cups in correspondence — the primal emotional dyad, exchange and reciprocity at their most elemental.

sections.Scene: The As de Coupes shows a single large chalice as the suit's foundational emblem. In the Italian pip tradition the cup ace is often the most ornate of the suit's pip cards, the single chalice given decorative treatment that marks it as the undivided potential of the emotional realm.
sections.Symbol: One cup as seed — the undivided emotional potential of the suit, receptivity held in its most concentrated and generative form.

sections.What it is: The Coupes (Cups) suit — ten pips plus Valet, Cavalier, Reine and Roi.
sections.Scene: The suit of Coupes (Cups) in the Paris anonymous tarot uses the Italian-suited chalice emblem with French court names: Roi, Reine, Cavalier, Valet. The pip cards follow standard Italian conventions with cup shapes in symmetrical distributions. Within a deck famous for its unusual trumps, the cups suit carries the more conventional iconography of the Italian pip tradition.
sections.Symbol: Cups as the suit of emotion, reception, and inner life — the chalice that holds feeling as surely as it holds liquid, within a deck that reimagines the world of the trumps while maintaining convention in its suits.

sections.Scene: The Roi de Bâtons is the King of Batons in the Italian-suited Paris anonymous tarot. He appears enthroned holding the baton or staff as his suit emblem, the king of wands in the Italian pip tradition adapted to the French court context. The baton king's authority is associated with fire and creative energy.
sections.Symbol: Baton King enthroned — creative authority and the governance of will and enterprise, the king who holds the staff as both weapon and sceptre.

sections.Scene: The Reine de Bâtons appears as the enthroned Queen of Batons, holding or displaying the staff emblem in the conventional seated-queen posture. She is the feminine sovereign of the suit of fire and creative enterprise in the Paris anonymous tarot's Italian-suited court hierarchy.
sections.Symbol: Baton Queen enthroned — the feminine mastery of creative will, the queen who holds the staff as emblem of her generative authority.

sections.Scene: The Cavalier de Bâtons is the mounted Knight of Batons, a horseman holding or brandishing the staff. The mounted knight of the baton suit in the Italian pip tradition embodies fiery forward momentum — the wand-bearer whose charge carries creative force into active expression.
sections.Symbol: Baton Knight on horseback — creative impulsion in motion, the will that charges forward carrying its staff as both weapon and wand.

sections.Scene: The Valet de Bâtons is the standing Page of Batons, a young figure holding or presenting the staff. The valet of the baton suit is the suit's most youthful and immediate expression — a page who carries the wand's creative potential without yet having directed it toward a specific enterprise.
sections.Symbol: Baton Valet standing — youth in possession of creative potential, the apprentice of enterprise and will.

sections.Scene: The Dix de Bâtons shows ten staff shapes in the dense interlocking arrangement typical of the baton suit's highest pip card. In the Italian-suited convention the ten batons are crowded and overlapping, the suit's energy accumulated to a point of burdensome fullness. The ten-baton card is the most heavily loaded of the suit's pip compositions.
sections.Symbol: Ten batons in dense interlace — creative energy at its most burdensome accumulation, the suit's force piled to its maximum weight.

sections.Scene: The Neuf de Bâtons shows nine staff shapes approaching the density of the ten. The nine batons carry the suit near its maximum load without yet completing it, an arrangement that suggests energy still active and unresolved rather than simply accumulated.
sections.Symbol: Nine batons approaching density — creative force near its peak, the suit's energy gathered toward its final expression.

sections.Scene: The Huit de Bâtons shows eight staff shapes in a symmetrical crossing arrangement. The even number allows the batons to form a geometric pattern, the eight staves crossing in pairs or groups to create an organized visual field that suggests the suit's energy channeled rather than merely accumulated.
sections.Symbol: Eight batons in crossing arrangement — creative force organized through geometry, the suit's energy structured into active pattern.

sections.Scene: The Sept de Bâtons shows seven staff shapes in a slightly asymmetric arrangement, the odd number preventing perfect bilateral symmetry. The seven batons in contest or in slight imbalance suggest an active, contested moment within the suit's energetic field.
sections.Symbol: Seven batons in active imbalance — creative tension at its most dynamic, the odd number that forces engagement.

sections.Scene: The Six de Bâtons shows six staff shapes in a balanced arrangement, the even distribution of the baton suit at its mid-range. Six staves crossed or arranged in pairs create a composition that suggests equilibrium rather than contest within the suit's domain of creative energy.
sections.Symbol: Six batons in balanced arrangement — creative equilibrium, the suit's fire in a moment of stable and organized correspondence.

sections.Scene: The Cinq de Bâtons shows five staff shapes with the characteristic tension of the suit's odd-number pip cards. Five batons in a contest arrangement suggest active encounter — the suit's energy at a point of competitive engagement, the five staves implied in motion against each other.
sections.Symbol: Five batons in contest arrangement — creative contest and vitality, the suit at its most dynamically engaged point.

sections.Scene: The Quatre de Bâtons shows four staff shapes in a square or cross formation. The four-baton card is among the most stable of the suit's pip compositions — four staves at four corners suggest the foundation of creative enterprise, a structure that supports further building.
sections.Symbol: Four batons in cross or square — creative foundation and structure, the suit's four-fold form as a basis for enterprise.

sections.Scene: The Trois de Bâtons shows three staff shapes in a triangular arrangement. The three batons suggest creative momentum — three staves in relation rather than mere pairing, the triangle of force that marks the beginning of organized enterprise.
sections.Symbol: Three batons in triangular relation — the beginning of creative momentum, three points of force defining direction.

sections.Scene: The Deux de Bâtons shows two staff shapes in crossing or parallel correspondence. The two batons are the suit's primal pairing — two wands in initial relation, the minimal expression of creative force meeting itself or its complement.
sections.Symbol: Two batons in initial crossing — the first meeting of creative forces, the suit's energy at its most elemental dyadic form.

sections.Scene: The As de Bâtons shows a single large staff as the suit's foundational emblem. In the Italian pip tradition the baton ace is typically the most unadorned and direct expression of the suit — a single wand or staff, the creative force undivided.
sections.Symbol: One baton as seed — the undivided creative force of the suit, fire and will before they divide into contest or enterprise.

sections.What it is: The Bâtons (Batons) suit — ten pips plus Valet, Cavalier, Reine and Roi.
sections.Scene: The suit of Bâtons (Batons) in the Paris anonymous tarot uses the Italian-suited staff or wand emblem with French court names: Roi, Reine, Cavalier, Valet. The pip cards follow standard Italian conventions with stave shapes in crossing and symmetrical arrangements. Within a deck famous for its unusual trumps, the batons suit carries the more conventional iconography of the Italian pip tradition.
sections.Symbol: Batons as the suit of fire, will, and creative enterprise — the staff that strikes and seeds, within a deck that reserves its most daring reimagination for the world of the major arcana.

sections.Scene: The Roi d'Épées is the King of Swords in the Italian-suited Paris anonymous tarot. He appears enthroned with a long sword as his suit emblem, the king of blades in the Italian pip tradition. The sword king's authority is associated with intellect, judgment, and the power of decisive action.
sections.Symbol: Sword King enthroned — intellectual authority and the governance of judgment, the king who holds the blade as both tool of discernment and emblem of command.

sections.Scene: The Reine d'Épées appears as the enthroned Queen of Swords, holding or displaying the sword emblem in the conventional seated-queen posture. She is the feminine sovereign of the suit of thought and discernment in the Paris anonymous tarot's Italian-suited court hierarchy.
sections.Symbol: Sword Queen enthroned — the feminine mastery of intellectual clarity, the queen who holds the blade as emblem of her discerning authority.

sections.Scene: The Cavalier d'Épées is the mounted Knight of Swords, a horseman charging with sword raised or extended. The sword knight's mounted posture embodies the suit's decisive, cutting energy in motion — the blade carried forward on horseback as an act of swift judgment or attack.
sections.Symbol: Sword Knight charging — intellectual impulsion in motion, the blade of discernment carried forward at speed.

sections.Scene: The Valet d'Épées is the standing Page of Swords, a young figure holding or presenting the sword. The valet of the sword suit is the suit's youthful bearer — a page who carries the blade's potential for precision and challenge without yet having exercised it in full.
sections.Symbol: Sword Valet standing — youth in possession of the blade, the apprentice of judgment and discernment.

sections.Scene: The Dix d'Épées shows ten sword shapes in the conventional Italian pip arrangement. In the Italian-suited tradition the ten of swords is among the densest pip compositions, the ten blades interlocking or crossing in a field that suggests the suit's accumulated cutting force at its maximum.
sections.Symbol: Ten swords in dense field — the suit's maximum cutting force, mental energy or conflict accumulated to its limit.

sections.Scene: The Neuf d'Épées shows nine sword shapes approaching the density of the ten. The nine blades fill the card's field without yet reaching the ten's completeness, the suit's accumulated sharpness near its peak.
sections.Symbol: Nine swords approaching density — the suit near its maximum, mental force gathered toward its most concentrated expression.

sections.Scene: The Huit d'Épées shows eight sword shapes in a symmetrical arrangement. The even number allows the eight blades to form balanced crossing patterns, the suit's energy organized through geometric symmetry in the Italian pip convention.
sections.Symbol: Eight swords in symmetric arrangement — the suit's energy organized through bilateral structure, discernment channeled into pattern.

sections.Scene: The Sept d'Épées shows seven sword shapes in a slightly asymmetric arrangement. Seven blades resist perfect bilateral symmetry, giving the card an active, slightly off-balance quality that suggests contest or alert readiness within the suit's intellectual domain.
sections.Symbol: Seven swords in slight imbalance — the suit at an active point of tension, seven blades that resist symmetry and suggest readiness.

sections.Scene: The Six d'Épées shows six sword shapes in a balanced arrangement of the Italian pip convention. The six blades are distributed in pairs or columns, the even number allowing a harmonious and readable composition within the suit's field of cutting clarity.
sections.Symbol: Six swords in balanced pairs — the suit at equilibrium, discernment held in a stable and organized arrangement.

sections.Scene: The Cinq d'Épées shows five sword shapes with the characteristic asymmetry of odd-number pip cards. Five blades arranged with a central dominant sword suggest contested division — the suit's energy at a point of active conflict rather than settled arrangement.
sections.Symbol: Five swords in contested arrangement — the suit's active conflict, five blades that cannot achieve symmetry and therefore remain in dynamic tension.

sections.Scene: The Quatre d'Épées shows four sword shapes in a square or cross formation. The four-sword card is among the more stable pip compositions of the suit — four blades at four points suggest rest or structured positioning, the suit's energy held in a firm four-fold arrangement.
sections.Symbol: Four swords in cross or square — the suit in its most stable and organized expression, blades held in structured rest.

sections.Scene: The Trois d'Épées shows three sword shapes crossing at or near the card's center. Three blades in crossing or triangular arrangement create the suit's minimal field of intersection — three points of sharpness in relation, the first multi-point crossing of the Italian sword pip.
sections.Symbol: Three swords at central crossing — the suit's minimal conflict made spatial, three blades whose intersection defines both tension and the possibility of resolution.

sections.Scene: The Deux d'Épées shows two sword shapes crossed in X formation. The two blades in crossing are the suit's primal opposition — the simplest visible expression of the conflict, judgment, and discernment that define the sword suit in the Italian pip tradition.
sections.Symbol: Two swords crossed — the primal opposition, conflict and discernment reduced to their most elemental visible form.

sections.Scene: The As d'Épées shows a single large sword as the suit's foundational emblem. In the Italian pip tradition the sword ace is typically bold and heraldic, the single blade — often shown with a crown or decorative surround — as the undivided potential of the suit's cutting clarity.
sections.Symbol: One sword as seed — the undivided force of the suit, discernment and clarity before they divide into conflict or judgment.

sections.What it is: The Épées (Swords) suit — ten pips plus Valet, Cavalier, Reine and Roi.
sections.Scene: The suit of Épées (Swords) in the Paris anonymous tarot uses the Italian-suited long sword emblem with French court names: Roi, Reine, Cavalier, Valet. The pip cards follow standard Italian conventions with blade shapes in crossing and symmetrical arrangements. The sword suit's Italian-tradition iconography carries the conventional pip vocabulary within a deck whose trumps are among the most unusual in the Marseille tradition.
sections.Symbol: Swords as the suit of thought, conflict, and discernment — the blade that cuts clean, within a deck that applies its own cutting intellect to the reimagination of the entire trump sequence.

sections.About: The unnumbered Fool, last in the BnF scan.

sections.Scene: Le Mat is the unnumbered Fool of the Paris anonymous deck — a vagabond or wandering figure broadly comparable to the Marseille Fool with his bundle and torn clothes. The Paris Fool occupies the conventional social position of the outcast without rank who travels outside the sequence of numbered trumps, his freedom from number signaling his exemption from the structured world the other cards represent.
sections.Symbol: Unnumbered vagabond Fool — freedom from rank and sequence, the figure who travels beyond the world's counting and therefore beyond its constraints.

ORIGIN OF THE SYMBOLISM OF THE UNNUMBERED CARD OF

THE TAROT.

The Shin 1 expresses the same hieroglyphic meaning as
the Zain (7th arcanum) and the Samech (15th) : this is an
arrow, an object directed to an aim. But the movement
which was direct in the Zain (*), and which became circular
in the Samech (p), here takes the form of a vibration from
one pole to the other, with an unstable point of equilibrium
in the centre. The Shin is therefore the sign of relative
duration and of the movement relating to it, whilst the

TWENTY-FIRST (UNNUMBERED) CARD OF THE TAROT.

The Foolish Man.

A careless-looking man, wearing a fool's cap, with torn
clothes and a bundle upon his shoulder, goes quietly on
his way, paying no attention to a dog which bites his leg.
He does not look where he is going, so walks towards a
precipice, where a crocodile is waiting to devour him.

This is an image of the state to which unresisted passion
will reduce a man. It is the symbol of the Flesh and of
its gratification. From a moral point of view the follow-
ing verses of Eliphas Levi well explain this symbol —

" Souffrir c'est travailler, c'est accomplir sa tâche,
Malheur au paresseux qui dort sur le chemin ;
La douleur, comme un chien, mord les talons du lâche,
Qui, d'un seul jour perdu, surcharge un lendemain." l

sections.What it is: The 22 named trumps — 21 numbered atouts plus the Mat.
sections.Scene: A navigational axis grouping all twenty-two Paris anonymous trump cards together. Among the most celebrated departures from the Marseille tradition: a goose-drawn chariot with child rider at VII, a Janus-faced Justice at VIII, a scholar measuring the heavens for the Star, a romantic serenade for the Moon, and a monkey vanity scene for the Sun. The Paris anonymous tarot is one of the earliest and most distinctive surviving French tarots.
sections.Symbol: The twenty-two major arcana of the Paris Anonymous tarot — a field of early 17th-century Parisian reimagination, where the Marseille vocabulary is inherited and then transformed through satire, science, and the comic inversion of triumph.
